label: Recorded real run — no models are running.

session.title: Choose the right workspace for Aiki

decision.brief: The user wants help choosing between an Electron desktop app and an `aiki serve` local web interface, then defining standout features and a practical development plan.
constraints: Orchestrate locally installed Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini/Antigravity CLIs.
Make orchestration visible in a high-quality browser interface.
Choose one delivery approach and provide a practical hackathon plan.

warnings.message: No person confirmed the interpreted request; documented defaults were used.
The supplied hackathon page blocked automated access with HTTP 403, so a source-investigation search was attempted.
Source investigation did not produce a cited public source; treat current claims as ungrounded.

result.headline: Build `aiki serve` — a local web cockpit that makes the model council visible, not an Electron app
result.bottomLine: Ship `aiki serve`: a loopback web UI launched from the CLI you already publish. It reuses the existing orchestration runtime and npm distribution, so a judge runs one command and opens a browser — no desktop build, code-signing, auto-update, or main-to-renderer IPC to fight. Electron would add all of that packaging work for no user-visible payoff on a hackathon timeline. Your edge is not multi-model chat — it is making the council itself the product surface.

sections.heading: The call: `aiki serve` over Electron
sections.summary: Recommend the local web server because it stands on what already exists and removes an entire class of delivery work.
bullets: Reuse the existing npm package and orchestration core; the browser talks to a Node server on loopback.
Avoid desktop signing, notarization, auto-update, and per-OS packaging.
Treat the event seam as the first technical gate, not an assumed capability.

sections.heading: What makes it stand out
sections.summary: The memorable moment is watching a bounded council become an auditable decision, not reading three answers.
bullets: Flight Deck: structured stage, role, status, elapsed-time, and artifact metadata.
Disagreement Radar: agreement, contradiction, missing evidence, and judge resolution.
Provenance on final sections without exposing hidden chain-of-thought.

sections.heading: The golden path to build first
sections.summary: One vertical slice: compose evidence, watch the council, get a traceable answer.
bullets: Keep scope to one existing workflow until structured events and a full live run are proven.
Use a clearly labelled deterministic replay when provider CLIs are unavailable.

sections.heading: Prove it before you polish
sections.summary: Three checks gate the build: structured events, a read-only loopback boundary, and a reliable full run on the demo machine.
bullets: Never build the activity view by parsing terminal prose.
Expose fixed Aiki workflows only; no arbitrary shell endpoint.
Confirm that the inspectable council is more useful than the terminal workflow before broadening scope.

wont: User accounts, cloud sync, and multi-user collaboration
Plugin system and arbitrary user-defined workflows

milestones.outcome: A safe working vertical slice that proves the structured-event seam and read-only loopback boundary.
The decision cockpit with evidence input, structured activity, provenance, and one report export.
A resilient showcase with preflight, explicit failure states, recorded replay, accessibility, and responsive layout.

result.nextStep: Run a focused spike that renders a live stage graph for one existing workflow directly from structured orchestration events; if it needs terminal-text parsing, redesign the seam before building UI.
caveats: The hackathon deadline, rules, and judging criteria were not verified because the supplied page returned HTTP 403.
Whether the core exposes every required structured event is not yet proven.
The claim that an inspectable council beats the existing terminal workflow remains unverified.

audit.summary: No genuine disagreements were stored. Convergence with weak verification lowers confidence; it does not raise it.

receipt.note: This completed run resumed an earlier partial run; this receipt contains only the two new calls recorded in the canonical run.
